title: ‘Hamnet’ and the 10 Best Performances of 2025. Plus: Kleber Mendonça Filho on ‘The Secret Agent.’
format: Sean, Amanda, and Joanna Robinson review Chloe Zhao's Hamnet and Kleber Mendonça Filho's The Secret Agent, then each count down ten favorite performances of the year. Closes with Sean's interview with Filho.

segments.heading: News before the reviews
segments.note: a Spielberg billboard and a Supergirl trailer

films.title: Disclosure
films.note: The new Steven Spielberg film, title unconfirmed, dated June 12, 2026. Josh O'Connor, Emily Blunt, Colman Domingo, Colin Firth, Wyatt Russell; rumored (the hosts doubt it) to be a Close Encounters quasi-sequel.

films.title: Supergirl
films.note: Second film in the new DCU, Craig Gillespie directing, Milly Alcock in the lead and Jason Momoa as Lobo. The read on the trailer: looks standard when it needs to look special.

films.title: Hamnet
films.note: Zhao's first film since Eternals, from O'Farrell's novel about Shakespeare's family and the death of his son. The table splits: a woo-woo, tell-don't-show first 90 minutes, then a final 20 in the Globe that flattens all three of them. Jessie Buckley carries it; Paul Mescal, Noah Jupe, and Jacobi Jupe get singled out. On the Nature of Daylight on the soundtrack is called a cheat.

films.title: The Secret Agent
films.note: A 160-minute genre-mixer near the top of Sean's list, comped to One Battle After Another and to Paul Thomas Anderson. Wagner Moura's performance lands fully only once the ending recontextualizes it. Amanda didn't love the film but loves the hang and the sense of place.

segments.heading: An actor you have a big relationship with

picks.title: Paddington in Peru
picks.note: Olivia Colman as a nun borrowing heavily from The Sound of Music. The first film Amanda took her son to in a theater.

picks.title: Blue Moon
picks.note: Ethan Hawke as a composer near the end of his life, in a performance Sean says is unlike anything we've seen from Hawke before.

segments.heading: The 10 best performances of 2025
segments.note: three critics, ten each, counted down 10 to 1

picks.title: Jane Austen Wrecked My Life
picks.note: No. 10. Camille Rutherford anchoring a small French film that feels like a cozy-but-real '90s indie.

picks.title: Now You See Me: Now You Don't
picks.note: No. 10, the villain-corridor trio, part one: Rosamund Pike as a South African diamond princess going all the way with the accent.

picks.title: The Running Man
picks.note: No. 10, part two: Colman Domingo as a preposterous, charismatic bad guy in the great tradition of accomplished actors funding a new wing on the house.

picks.title: F1
picks.note: No. 10, part three: Tobias Menzies, another utterly enjoyable villain who gets his one scene.

picks.title: Friendship
picks.note: No. 10, comic relief part one: Conor O'Malley berating Tim Robinson in the garage, one of Sean's favorite things all year.

picks.title: The Naked Gun
picks.note: No. 10, comic relief part two: Danny Huston committing fully, funny opposite Pamela Anderson.

picks.title: The Phoenician Scheme
picks.note: No. 9: Michael Cera, born for the Wes Anderson role, alongside his Running Man turn.

picks.title: Companion
picks.note: No. 9: Jack Quaid, both Meg Ryan incarnate and his own person, holding the movie's pathos and smarm.

picks.title: One Battle After Another
picks.note: No. 9 (swapped to avoid overlap): Chase Infiniti, a real comeback from the worst TV twist to a performance that takes the movie away from Penn, del Toro, and DiCaprio.

picks.title: Freaky Tales
picks.note: No. 8: Normani and Dominique Thorne in the rap-battle vignette, the best thing in a shaggy movie.

picks.title: Mickey 17
picks.note: No. 8: Robert Pattinson taking the biggest voice swing; the movie didn't fully work but he did.

picks.title: Sentimental Value
picks.note: No. 8: Inga Ibsdóttir Lilleaas as Renate Reinsve's sister, the most real person in a movie full of celebrated actors.

picks.title: Kiss of the Spider Woman
picks.note: No. 7: Tonatiuh as Luis Molina, handsome and charismatic in the William Hurt role, narrating nearly the whole film.

picks.title: One of Them Days
picks.note: No. 7: Keke Palmer and SZA, whose chemistry Amanda wishes they'd franchise Bob-Hope-style.

picks.title: Rebuilding
picks.note: No. 7, Josh O'Connor writ large, part one: a quiet, affecting turn Sean underlines.

picks.title: The Mastermind
picks.note: No. 7, Josh O'Connor writ large, part two.

picks.title: The History of Sound
picks.note: No. 7, Josh O'Connor writ large, part three: a film Sean didn't care for but thinks O'Connor is good enough in.

picks.title: Marty Supreme
picks.note: No. 6: Odessa A'zion as Marty's childhood friend, funny and sexy and load-bearing for the plot.

picks.title: Black Bag
picks.note: No. 6: Tom Burke, the right amount of charming and amused among Blanchett and Fassbender, starting as villain and evolving.

picks.title: Wake Up Dead Man: A Knives Out Mystery
picks.note: No. 5 for Joanna, No. 3 for Amanda, part of Sean's O'Connor bundle: Josh O'Connor working through a Knives Out movie, the one Joanna will think about longest.

picks.title: A House of Dynamite
picks.note: No. 5 (Amanda): Rebecca Ferguson in the best, first third, an un-flashy performance that sets impossible stakes.

picks.title: It Was Just an Accident
picks.note: No. 5 (Sean): Mariam Afshari as Shiva in the Panahi film, the best-written character and the deepest pain in the story, performed in secret.

picks.title: Nouvelle Vague
picks.note: No. 4: Guillaume Marbeck as Godard and Aubry Dullin as Belmondo, neither an actor before, Marbeck the one who carries it.

picks.title: Mission: Impossible - The Final Reckoning
picks.note: No. 4: Tramell Tillman and Katy O'Brian, the only two who seem to know what the movie should be, bringing levity to a film tied up in itself.

picks.title: Highest 2 Lowest
picks.note: No. 4, part one: A$AP Rocky as pure charisma trading verses with Denzel.

picks.title: Lurker
picks.note: No. 4, part two: Archie Madekwe as a Drake/Dijon-adjacent star, a well-observed study of power in a small room, and one of the year's coolest debuts.

picks.title: Twinless
picks.note: No. 3: Dylan O'Brien in a dual role, a straight slacker and a mean gay twin, convincing at both.

picks.title: The Testament of Ann Lee
picks.note: No. 3 (Sean): Amanda Seyfried as a cult founder, all-in on the singing and the seriousness that make Mona Fastvold's movie work.

picks.title: Sorry, Baby
picks.note: No. 2: Eva Victor, who wrote, directed, and stars, hitting serious, absurd, and devastating in one movie. A newly minted Golden Globe nominee.

picks.title: If I Had Legs I'd Kick You
picks.note: No. 2, the two moms, part one: Rose Byrne, raw and recognizable.

picks.title: Die My Love
picks.note: No. 2, the two moms, part two: Jennifer Lawrence in an overlooked movie Amanda loved.

picks.title: No Other Choice
picks.note: No. 2 (Sean): Lee Byung-hun in the Park Chan-wook satire, funny, desperate, sad, violent, and crazed, sometimes in one scene.

picks.title: K-Pop Demon Hunters
picks.note: No. 2, Lee Byung-hun's other 2025 turn: the lead mountain demon.

picks.title: Hamnet
picks.note: No. 1 (Joanna and Amanda, shared): Jessie Buckley, who carries the film's final hour and whom Sean calls one of his favorite actresses alive.

picks.title: Marty Supreme
picks.note: No. 1 (Sean): Timothée Chalamet, self-confident on the trail, playing the wrestling-and-entertainment intersection the movie is built on.

films.title: The Secret Agent
films.note: Written specifically for Wagner Moura, no plan B. Filho on shooting the St. Louis cinema like an actor, split-diopter shots borrowed from Blow Out and Close Encounters, and the Udo Kier sequence about identity and assumption.

films.title: Pictures of Ghosts
films.note: The documentary/film essay that put him in the mood to write The Secret Agent; both are memory movies about Recife filmgoing.

films.title: Bacurau
films.note: His previous fiction feature, also with Udo Kier, and a touchstone for the genre-cinema and John Carpenter love.

films.title: Neighboring Sounds
films.note: His first feature, written in 2008 and shot in 2010; the film Wagner Moura saw before reaching out to work together.

films.title: Aquarius
films.note: A contemporary story where he'd already used split-diopter shots, a tool he says just went out of fashion.

Jeanne Dielman, 23 quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles
